label: Formula 18.15a
subsection: Weighted Least-squares Estimates of Realized Heritability
nearby_text: _e^2 \cdot \mathbf{I} $, and generalized least-squares (GLS) must be used to account for the overall covariance structure. From LW Equation 8.34, the GLS estimator of the regression slope is given by $$ \widehat{b}_{C}(\mathrm{GLS})=(\mathbf{S}^{T}\mathbf{V}^{-1}\mathbf{S})^{-1}\mathbf{S}^{T}\mathbf{V}^{-1}\mathbf{R} $$ (18.15a) where V is the variance-covariance matrix associated with selection response, with elements $$ V_{i j}=\sigma_{e}(i,j)=\sigma\left[R_{C}(i),R_{C}(j)\right] $$ (18.15b)

label: Formula 18.16b
subsection: Standard Errors of Realized Heritability Estimates
nearby_text: The residual variance, $ \sigma_{e}^{2} $, can be estimated from the residual sums of squares divided by the degrees of freedom (see LW Chapter 8). For an experiment lasting for T generations $$ \widehat{\sigma}_{e}^{2}=\frac{1}{T-1}\sum_{i=1}^{T}\widehat{e}_{i}^{2}=\frac{1}{T-1}\sum_{i=1}^{T}\left(R_{C}(i)-\widehat{h}_{r}^{2}S_{C}(i)\right)^{2} $$ (18.16b) As mentioned, because the OLS estimator assumes that residuals are uncorrelated and have equal variances (both of which are incorrect under drift), it significantly underestimates the correct varianc

label: Formula 18.17
subsection: Standard Errors of Realized Heritability Estimates
nearby_text: e GLS regression estimator (Equation 18.15) avoids these problems by properly accounting for the residual variance-covariance structure generated by drift. From standard GLS theory (LW Equation 8.35) $$ \mathrm{Var}\left[\widehat{b}_{C}(\mathrm{GLS})\right]=(\mathbf{S}^{T}\mathbf{V}^{-1}\mathbf{S})^{-1} $$ (18.17) As was done above, the pure-drift approximation is used to obtain the elements of V, with $ \widehat{h}_{r}^{2} $ used in place of $ h^{2} $.

label: Formula 18.18
subsection: Standard Errors of Realized Heritability Estimates
nearby_text: Finally, consider the variance for the ratio estimator $ b_T $, the total response to total selection (Equation 18.12). Because $ \text{Var}(y/c) = \text{Var}(y)/c^2 $ for a constant, c, we have $$ \mathrm{Var}(\widehat{b}_{T})=\frac{\mathrm{Var}\left[R_{C}(T)\right]}{S_{C}^{2}(T)}\simeq\frac{(T/N)\widehat{h}_{r}^{2}\sigma_{z}^{2}+\sigma_{z}^{2}/M_{T}}{S_{C}^{2}(T)} $$ (18.18)

  "nearby_text": "Finally, consider the variance for the ratio estimator $ b_T $, the total response to total selection (Equation 18.12). Because $ \\text{Var}(y/c) = \\text{Var}(y)/c^2 $ for a constant, c, we have $$ \\mathrm{Var}(\\widehat{b}_{T})=\\frac{\\mathrm{Var}\\left[R_{C}(T)\\right]}{S_{C}^{2}(T)}\\simeq\\frac{(T/N)\\widehat{h}_{r}^{2}\\sigma_{z}^{2}+\\sigma_{z}^{2}/M_{T}}{S_{C}^{2}(T)} $$ (18.18)"
}

label: Formula 18.19
subsection: Power: Estimation of $ h^{2} $ from Relatives or Selection Response?
nearby_text: t the strength of selection is the same in each generation $ (S_i = S) $, in which case we have that $$ S_{C}^{2}(T)/\sigma_{z}^{2}=T^{2}\; S^{2}/\sigma_{z}^{2}=T^{2}\cdot\bar{\imath}^{2} $$ and hence $$ \mathrm{Var}(\widehat{h}_{r}^{2})\simeq\frac{(T/N)\widehat{h}_{r}^{2}+1/M}{T^{2}\cdot\overline{\imath}^{2}}=\frac{(1/TN)\widehat{h}_{r}^{2}+1/(T^{2}M)}{\overline{\imath}^{2}}\simeq\frac{\widehat{h}_{r}^{2}}{TN\overline{\imath}^{2}} $$ (18.19) where the last step follows by ignoring the $ 1/(T^{2}M) $ term.

subsection: Infinitesimal-model Corrections for Disequilibrium
nearby_text: majority of this decrease occurs over the first few generations, after which the heritability is essentially at its equilibrium value, $ \widetilde{h}^{2} $, where (from Equations 16.13a and 16.13c) $$ \begin{align*}\widetilde h^2=\frac{\gamma}{1+\gamma-h^2}\quad{\rm with}\quad\gamma=\frac{2h^2-1+\sqrt{1+4h^2(1-h^2)\kappa}}{2(1+\kappa)}\end{align*} $$ (18.21)

label: Formula 18.24a
subsection: Basic Theory of Control Populations
nearby_text: E[g_{c,t}] = 0 $. If we assume that there are no genotype × environment interactions (i.e., $ b_{s,t} = b_{c,t} $), the contrast between the selected and control populations has an expected value of $$ E[\overline{z}_{s,t}-\overline{z}_{c,t}]=E[g_{s,t}]-E[g_{c,t}]=h^{2}S_{C}(t) $$ (18.24a)

label: Formula 18.27d
subsection: Divergent Selection Designs
nearby_text: ction differentials) is simply $ R = h^{2}S $. More generally, if $ S_{x,i} $ denotes the selection differential in line x at generation i, the total (cumulative) expected divergence between lines is $$ R_{C}(t)=h^{2}\sum_{i=1}^{t}\left(S_{u,i}-S_{d,i}\right)=h^{2}\left(S_{C,u}-S_{C,d}\right) $$ (18.27d)

label: Formula 18.28
subsection: Variance in Response
nearby_text: ons for the selection plus control and divergence selection designs (assuming there are no genotype × environmental interactions between lines). For unidirectional selection plus a control population $$ \begin{aligned}R_{C}(t)=\overline{z}_{s,t}-\overline{z}_{c,t}&=\left(\mu+g_{s,t}+b_{t}+e_{s,t}\right)-\left(\mu+g_{c,t}+b_{t}+e_{c,t}\right)\\&=\left(g_{s,t}-g_{c,t}\right)+\left(e_{s,t}-e_{c,t}\right)\end{aligned} $$ (18.28)

}

label: Formula 18.31a
subsection: Control Populations and Variance in Response
nearby_text: Equation 18.30a, along with the coefficients from Table 18.7, gives the expected variance with and without the use of a control. Assuming $ M = M_s = M_c $ and $ N = N_s = N_c $, this equation yields $$ \sigma^{2}\left[R_{C}(t)\right]_{control}-\sigma^{2}\left[R_{C}(t)\right]_{no control}=\left(\frac{t}{N}+\frac{1}{M_{0}}\right)h^{2}\sigma_{z}^{2}+\frac{1}{M}\sigma_{z}^{2}-\sigma_{b}^{2} $$ (18.31a)

        "latex": "R_{C}(t)=h^{2}\\sum_{i=1}^{t}\\left(S_{u,i}-S_{d,i}\\right)=h^{2}\\left(S_{C,u}-S_{C,d}\\right)",

        "id": "formula_18.28",
        "latex": "\\begin{aligned}R_{C}(t)=\\overline{z}_{s,t}-\\overline{z}_{c,t}&=\\left(\\mu+g_{s,t}+b_{t}+e_{s,t}\\right)-\\left(\\mu+g_{c,t}+b_{t}+e_{c,t}\\right)\\\\&=\\left(g_{s,t}-g_{c,t}\\right)+\\left(e_{s,t}-e_{c,t}\\right)\\end{aligned}",

        "latex": "R_{C}(t)=\\overline{z}_{s u,t}-\\overline{z}_{s d,t}=\\left(g_{s u,t}-g_{s d,t}\\right)+\\left(e_{s u,t}-e_{s d,t}\\right)",

        "latex": "\\sigma^{2}\\left[R_{C}(t)\\right]=\\left(2f_{t}+B_{0}\\right)h^{2}\\sigma_{z}^{2}+B_{t}\\sigma_{z}^{2}\\simeq\\left(t A+B_{0}\\right)h^{2}\\sigma_{z}^{2}+B_{t}\\sigma_{z}^{2}",
        "label": "Formula 18.30a",
        "section": "CONTROL POPULATIONS AND EXPERIMENTAL DESIGNS",
        "subsection": "Variance in Response",
        "position": 57,
        "symbols_defined": [

        "label": "Formula 18.30b",
        "section": "CONTROL POPULATIONS AND EXPERIMENTAL DESIGNS",
        "subsection": "Variance in Response",
        "position": 58,
        "symbols_defined": [

  "nearby_text": "Equation 18.30a, along with the coefficients from Table 18.7, gives the expected variance with and without the use of a control. Assuming $ M = M_s = M_c $ and $ N = N_s = N_c $, this equation yields $$ \\sigma^{2}\\left[R_{C}(t)\\right]_{control}-\\sigma^{2}\\left[R_{C}(t)\\right]_{no control}=\\left(\\frac{t}{N}+\\frac{1}{M_{0}}\\right)h^{2}\\sigma_{z}^{2}+\\frac{1}{M}\\sigma_{z}^{2}-\\sigma_{b}^{2} $$ (18.31a)"
}

label: Formula 18.31b
subsection: Control Populations and Variance in Response
nearby_text: ing the between-line drift variance dominates (terms involving M can be ignored), the condition for the variance in response with a control to be larger than the response without one is approximately $$ t\sigma_{z}^{2}h^{2}/N>\sigma_{b}^{2} $$ (18.31b)

label: Formula 18.32a
subsection: Control Populations and Variance in Response
nearby_text: Selection in a single direction with a control line. $$ E\left[R_{C}(t)\right]=t h^{2}\bar{\imath}\sigma_{z},\qquad\mathrm{C V}\left[R_{C}(t)\right]\simeq\frac{1}{h\bar{\imath}}\sqrt{\frac{2}{N t}} $$ (18.32a)

  "nearby_text": "As Equation 18.31b illustrates, it is not entirely obvious which design is optimal. What in general can we say? The coefficient of variation (CV) of the selection response $$ \\mathrm{CV}\\left[R_{C}(t)\\right]=\\frac{\\sigma\\left[R_{C}(t)\\right]}{E\\left[R_{C}(t)\\right]} $$ (18.33) is especially useful in comparing efficiencies of different designs, as it is independent of $ \\sigma_{z}^{2} $. Further, it provides an appropriate measure of comparative efficiencies when the expec"
}

label: Formula 18.34
subsection: Nicholas' Criterion
nearby_text: ection is normally distributed, with a mean of $ E[R_C(t)] $ and a variance of $ \sigma^2[R_C(t)] $. Consider the probability that the observed response is at least $ \beta $ of the expected response $$ \begin{aligned}\Pr\left(R_{C}(t)>\beta E[R_{C}(t)]\right)&=\Pr\left(\frac{R_{C}(t)-E[R_{C}(t)]}{\sigma\left[R_{C}(t)\right]}>\frac{(\beta-1)E[R_{C}(t)]}{\sigma\left[R_{C}(t)\right]}\right)\\&=\Pr\left(U>\frac{\beta-1}{\mathbf{CV}\left[R_{C}(t)\right]}\right)\end{aligned} $$ (18.34) where $U$ is a unit normal random variable. Note that the probability that the observed response exceeds the expected response ($\beta = 1$) is one half, as $\Pr[U > 0] = 1/2$.

  "nearby_text": "ection is normally distributed, with a mean of $ E[R_C(t)] $ and a variance of $ \\sigma^2[R_C(t)] $. Consider the probability that the observed response is at least $ \\beta $ of the expected response $$ \\begin{aligned}\\Pr\\left(R_{C}(t)>\\beta E[R_{C}(t)]\\right)&=\\Pr\\left(\\frac{R_{C}(t)-E[R_{C}(t)]}{\\sigma\\left[R_{C}(t)\\right]}>\\frac{(\\beta-1)E[R_{C}(t)]}{\\sigma\\left[R_{C}(t)\\right]}\\right)\\\\&=\\Pr\\left(U>\\frac{\\beta-1}{\\mathbf{CV}\\left[R_{C}(t)\\right]}\\right)\\end{aligned} $$ (18.34) where $U$ is a unit normal random variable. Note that the probability that the observed response exceeds the expected response ($\\beta = 1$) is one half, as $\\Pr[U > 0] = 1/2$."
}

label: Formula 18.35a
subsection: Replicate Lines
nearby_text: iciency when replicate lines are used (Hill 1980). To see this, let $ \overline{z}_{i,t} $ for $ 1 \leq i \leq r $ be the sample mean for replicate population $ i $ at time $ t $. The overall mean is $$ \overline{z}_{t}=\frac{1}{r}\sum_{i=1}^{r}\overline{z}_{i,t} $$ (18.35a)

  "nearby_text": "iciency when replicate lines are used (Hill 1980). To see this, let $ \\overline{z}_{i,t} $ for $ 1 \\leq i \\leq r $ be the sample mean for replicate population $ i $ at time $ t $. The overall mean is $$ \\overline{z}_{t}=\\frac{1}{r}\\sum_{i=1}^{r}\\overline{z}_{i,t} $$ (18.35a)"
}

subsection: Replicate Lines

subsection: The Simple Additive Model
nearby_text: Summing over all loci, and recalling Equation 18.36, we get an expected mean for this cross of $$ C_{k\times j}=m+A\cdot\frac{k+j}{2} $$ (18.37b)

        "latex": "\\widehat{b}_{C}(\\mathrm{OLS})=\\left(\\mathbf{S}^{T}\\mathbf{S}\\right)^{-1}\\mathbf{S}^{T}\\mathbf{R}=\\frac{\\sum_{i=1}^{T}S_{C}(i)\\cdot R_{C}(i)}{\\sum_{i=1}^{T}S_{C}^{2}(i)}",

        "latex": "\\widehat{b}_{C}(\\mathrm{GLS})=(\\mathbf{S}^{T}\\mathbf{V}^{-1}\\mathbf{S})^{-1}\\mathbf{S}^{T}\\mathbf{V}^{-1}\\mathbf{R}",

        "label": "Formula 18.15c",

        "latex": "\\mathrm{Var}\\left[\\widehat{b}_{C}(\\mathrm{OLS})\\right]=\\sigma_{e}^{2}\\left(\\mathbf{X}^{T}\\mathbf{X}\\right)^{-1}=\\sigma_{e}^{2}\\left(\\mathbf{S}^{T}\\mathbf{S}\\right)^{-1}=\\sigma_{e}^{2}\\bigg/\\sum_{i=1}^{T}S_{C}^{2}(i)",

        "latex": "\\widehat{\\sigma}_{e}^{2}=\\frac{1}{T-1}\\sum_{i=1}^{T}\\widehat{e}_{i}^{2}=\\frac{1}{T-1}\\sum_{i=1}^{T}\\left(R_{C}(i)-\\widehat{h}_{r}^{2}S_{C}(i)\\right)^{2}",

        "latex": "\\mathrm{Var}(\\widehat{b}_{T})=\\frac{\\mathrm{Var}\\left[R_{C}(T)\\right]}{S_{C}^{2}(T)}\\simeq\\frac{(T/N)\\widehat{h}_{r}^{2}\\sigma_{z}^{2}+\\sigma_{z}^{2}/M_{T}}{S_{C}^{2}(T)}",

        "latex": "\\mathrm{Var}(\\widehat{h}_{r}^{2})\\simeq\\frac{(T/N)\\widehat{h}_{r}^{2}+1/M}{T^{2}\\cdot\\overline{\\imath}^{2}}=\\frac{(1/TN)\\widehat{h}_{r}^{2}+1/(T^{2}M)}{\\overline{\\imath}^{2}}\\simeq\\frac{\\widehat{h}_{r}^{2}}{TN\\overline{\\imath}^{2}}",
        "label": "Formula 18.19",

label: Formula 18.38a
subsection: The Hammond-Gardner Model
nearby_text: , \text{ and } D_{q}) $. Assuming all lines start from the same base population, as derived below in Example 18.10, the Hammond-Gardner (HG) model returns means for various lines and line crosses of: $$ C_{k}=m+A\cdot k+D_{0}+D\cdot k+D_{q}\cdot k^{2} $$ (18.38a) $$ C_{k}(s)=m+A\cdot k+\frac{1}{2}\left(D_{0}+D\cdot k+D_{q}\cdot k^{2}\right) $$ (18.38b) $$ C_{k\times j}=m+\frac{A}{2}(k+j)+D_{0}+\frac{1}{2}\left[D\cdot(k+j)+2D_{q}\cdot k j\right] $$ (18.38c) $$

        "latex": "\\widehat{b}_{C}(\\mathrm{OLS})=\\left(\\mathbf{S}^{T}\\mathbf{S}\\right)^{-1}\\mathbf{S}^{T}\\mathbf{R}=\\frac{\\sum_{i=1}^{T}S_{C}(i)\\cdot R_{C}(i)}{\\sum_{i=1}^{T}S_{C}^{2}(i)}",

        "latex": "\\mathrm{Var}\\left[\\widehat{b}_{C}(\\mathrm{OLS})\\right]=\\sigma_{e}^{2}\\left(\\mathbf{X}^{T}\\mathbf{X}\\right)^{-1}=\\sigma_{e}^{2}\\left(\\mathbf{S}^{T}\\mathbf{S}\\right)^{-1}=\\sigma_{e}^{2}\\bigg/\\sum_{i=1}^{T}S_{C}^{2}(i)",

        "latex": "\\mathrm{Var}(\\widehat{b}_{T})=\\frac{\\mathrm{Var}\\left[R_{C}(T)\\right]}{S_{C}^{2}(T)}\\simeq\\frac{(T/N)\\widehat{h}_{r}^{2}\\sigma_{z}^{2}+\\sigma_{z}^{2}/M_{T}}{S_{C}^{2}(T)}",

        "latex": "\\mathrm{Var}(\\widehat{h}_{r}^{2})\\simeq\\frac{(T/N)\\widehat{h}_{r}^{2}+1/M}{T^{2}\\cdot\\overline{\\imath}^{2}}=\\frac{(1/TN)\\widehat{h}_{r}^{2}+1/(T^{2}M)}{\\overline{\\imath}^{2}}\\simeq\\frac{\\widehat{h}_{r}^{2}}{TN\\overline{\\imath}^{2}}",
        "label": "Formula 18.19",

        "latex": "\\widehat{b}_{C}(\\mathrm{OLS})=\\left(\\mathbf{S}^{T}\\mathbf{S}\\right)^{-1}\\mathbf{S}^{T}\\mathbf{R}=\\frac{\\sum_{i=1}^{T}S_{C}(i)\\cdot R_{C}(i)}{\\sum_{i=1}^{T}S_{C}^{2}(i)}",

        "latex": "\\widehat{b}_{C}(\\mathrm{GLS})=(\\mathbf{S}^{T}\\mathbf{V}^{-1}\\mathbf{S})^{-1}\\mathbf{S}^{T}\\mathbf{V}^{-1}\\mathbf{R}",

        "label": "Formula 18.15c",

        "latex": "\\mathrm{Var}\\left[\\widehat{b}_{C}(\\mathrm{OLS})\\right]=\\sigma_{e}^{2}\\left(\\mathbf{X}^{T}\\mathbf{X}\\right)^{-1}=\\sigma_{e}^{2}\\left(\\mathbf{S}^{T}\\mathbf{S}\\right)^{-1}=\\sigma_{e}^{2}\\bigg/\\sum_{i=1}^{T}S_{C}^{2}(i)",

        "latex": "\\widehat{\\sigma}_{e}^{2}=\\frac{1}{T-1}\\sum_{i=1}^{T}\\widehat{e}_{i}^{2}=\\frac{1}{T-1}\\sum_{i=1}^{T}\\left(R_{C}(i)-\\widehat{h}_{r}^{2}S_{C}(i)\\right)^{2}",

        "latex": "\\mathrm{Var}(\\widehat{b}_{T})=\\frac{\\mathrm{Var}\\left[R_{C}(T)\\right]}{S_{C}^{2}(T)}\\simeq\\frac{(T/N)\\widehat{h}_{r}^{2}\\sigma_{z}^{2}+\\sigma_{z}^{2}/M_{T}}{S_{C}^{2}(T)}",

        "latex": "\\mathrm{Var}(\\widehat{h}_{r}^{2})\\simeq\\frac{(T/N)\\widehat{h}_{r}^{2}+1/M}{T^{2}\\cdot\\overline{\\imath}^{2}}=\\frac{(1/TN)\\widehat{h}_{r}^{2}+1/(T^{2}M)}{\\overline{\\imath}^{2}}\\simeq\\frac{\\widehat{h}_{r}^{2}}{TN\\overline{\\imath}^{2}}",
        "label": "Formula 18.19",

label: Formula 18.39c
subsection: The Hammond-Gardner Model
nearby_text: , k. Recalling (LW Equation 4.10b) that the average effect, $ \alpha $, of an allelic substitution (replacing an unfavorable allele by a favorable one) is given by $ \alpha = a + d(1 - 2p) $, we have $$ \begin{align*}A+D&=2\left(\sum\Delta p_i a_i+\sum\Delta p_i(1-2p_i)d_i\right)\\&=2\left(\sum\Delta p_i\left[a_i+(1-2p_i)d_i\right]\right)=2\sum\Delta p_i\alpha_i\end{align*} $$ (18.39c) with $ A+D $ representing the per-generation change in the trait mean as a linear regression on cycle number (e.g., $ k[A+D] $ for line $ k $). This is also twice the average effect of substituting t

Recalling (LW Equation 4.10b) that the average effect, $ \\alpha $, of an allelic substitution (replacing an unfavorable allele by a favorable one) is given by $ \\alpha = a + d(1 - 2p) $, we have $$ \\begin{align*}A+D&=2\\left(\\sum\\Delta p_i a_i+\\sum\\Delta p_i(1-2p_i)d_i\\right)\\\\&=2\\left(\\sum\\Delta p_i\\left[a_i+(1-2p_i)d_i\\right]\\right)=2\\sum\\Delta p_i\\alpha_i\\end{align*} $$ (18.39c) with $ A+D $ representing the per-generation change in the trait mean as a linear regression on cycle number (e.g., $ k[A+D] $ for line $ k $). This is also twice the average effect of substituting t"
}

  "nearby_text": "N_e) $ is the amount of inbreeding in cycle $ k $. Melchinger and Flachenecker suggested that, with selection, Equation 18.41a can be approximated by using the average frequency over the $ k $ cycles $$ E[(\\delta p_{k})^{2}]\\simeq\\left(\\frac{p(0)[1-p(0)]+p(k)[1-p(k)]}{2}\\right)f_{k} $$ (18.41b)"
}

latex: E[\left(\delta p_{k}\right)^{2}]\simeq p(0)[1-p(0)]\; f_{k}+\frac{k}{2}\cdot(1-2p)\Delta p\; f_{k}-\frac{k^{2}}{2}\left(\Delta p\right)^{2}\; f_{k}
label: Formula 18.41c
subsection: Accounting for Inbreeding Depression and Drift
nearby_text: Using the deterministic approximation $ p(k) = p(0) + k\Delta p $, this reduces to $$ E[\left(\delta p_{k}\right)^{2}]\simeq p(0)[1-p(0)]\; f_{k}+\frac{k}{2}\cdot(1-2p)\Delta p\; f_{k}-\frac{k^{2}}{2}\left(\Delta p\right)^{2}\; f_{k} $$ (18.41c)

        "latex": "\\begin{aligned}R_{C}(t)=\\overline{z}_{s,t}-\\overline{z}_{c,t}&=\\left(\\mu+g_{s,t}+b_{t}+e_{s,t}\\right)-\\left(\\mu+g_{c,t}+b_{t}+e_{c,t}\\right)\\\\&=\\left(g_{s,t}-g_{c,t}\\right)+\\left(e_{s,t}-e_{c,t}\\right)\\end{aligned}",

        "latex": "\\begin{aligned}\\Pr\\left(R_{C}(t)>\\beta E[R_{C}(t)]\\right)&=\\Pr\\left(\\frac{R_{C}(t)-E[R_{C}(t)]}{\\sigma\\left[R_{C}(t)\\right]}>\\frac{(\\beta-1)E[R_{C}(t)]}{\\sigma\\left[R_{C}(t)\\right]}\\right)\\\\&=\\Pr\\left(U>\\frac{\\beta-1}{\\mathbf{CV}\\left[R_{C}(t)\\right]}\\right)\\end{aligned}",

      {
        "id": "formula_18.39c",
        "latex": "\\begin{align*}A+D&=2\\left(\\sum\\Delta p_i a_i+\\sum\\Delta p_i(1-2p_i)d_i\\right)\\\\&=2\\left(\\sum\\Delta p_i\\left[a_i+(1-2p_i)d_i\\right]\\right)=2\\sum\\Delta p_i\\alpha_i\\end{align*}",
        "label": "Formula 18.39c",
        "section": "LINE-CROSS ANALYSIS OF SELECTION EXPERIMENTS",

        "id": "formula_18.39d",

      {
        "id": "formula_18.41a",
        "latex": "\\sigma^{2}(\\delta p_{k})=p(0)[1-p(0)]f_{k}",
        "label": "Formula 18.41a",
        "section": "LINE-CROSS ANALYSIS OF SELECTION EXPERIMENTS",
        "subsection": "Accounting for Inbreeding Depression and Drift",
        "position": 82,
        "symbols_defined": [

          "\\sigma",
          "k",
          "p"
        ]
      },
      {
        "id": "formula_18.41b",
        "latex": "E[(\\delta p_{k})^{2}]\\simeq\\left(\\frac{p(0)[1-p(0)]+p(k)[1-p(k)]}{2}\\right)f_{k}",
        "label": "Formula 18.41b",
        "section": "LINE-CROSS ANALYSIS OF SELECTION EXPERIMENTS",
        "subsection": "Accounting for Inbreeding Depression and Drift",
        "position": 83,
        "symbols_defined": [
          "E"
        ]
      }
    ]
  },
  "nearby_text": "Using the deterministic approximation $ p(k) = p(0) + k\\Delta p $, this reduces to $$ E[\\left(\\delta p_{k}\\right)^{2}]\\simeq p(0)[1-p(0)]\\; f_{k}+\\frac{k}{2}\\cdot(1-2p)\\Delta p\\; f_{k}-\\frac{k^{2}}{2}\\left(\\Delta p\\right)^{2}\\; f_{k} $$ (18.41c)"
}

label: Formula 18.42a
subsection: Accounting for Inbreeding Depression and Drift
nearby_text: $ (c.f. Equations 18.39a–18.39d). Using Equation 18.40 as the model for allele frequency and following the logic used in Example 18.10, the adjustment in the mean for inbreeding due to drift becomes $$ I_{k}=\left(D_{0}+\frac{k\cdot D}{2}-\frac{k^{2}\cdot D_{q}}{2}\right)f_{k} $$ (18.42a)

  "nearby_text": "$ (c.f. Equations 18.39a–18.39d). }

label: Formula 18.42b
subsection: Accounting for Inbreeding Depression and Drift
nearby_text: Assuming $ f_k = k \Delta f $ (typically, with $ \Delta f = 1/[2N_e] $), this simplifies to $$ I_{k}=k\left(D_{0}+\frac{k\cdot D}{2}-\frac{k^{2}\cdot D_{q}}{2}\right)\Delta f $$ (18.42b)

label: Formula 18.43a
subsection: Accounting for Inbreeding Depression and Drift
nearby_text: The resulting line means are $$ C_{k}=C_{k}(HG)-I_{k}\qquad and\qquad C_{k}(s)=C_{k}(s,HG)-I_{k}/2 $$ (18.43a) while the line-cross means become $$ C_{k\times j}=C_{k\times j}(HG)-I_{k}\quad for j>k $$ (18.43b) where HG denotes the value under the Hammond-Gardner model (Equations 18.38a–18.38e).

label: Formula 18.45b
subsection: Accounting for Inbreeding Depression and Drift
nearby_text: Drift dominates the expected quadratic change when $ \sigma^{2}(\delta p) \gg (\Delta p)^{2} $, or $$ \frac{p(1-p)}{2N_{e}}\gg\frac{p(1-p)}{2}\frac{\bar{\tau}^{2}h^{2}}{n} $$ which reduces to $$ n\gg N_{e}\left(\bar{\imath}^{2}h^{2}\right) $$ (18.45b)

}

label: Formula 18.45c
subsection: Accounting for Inbreeding Depression and Drift
nearby_text: er than the effective population size (assuming loci have roughly equal effects). Expressed in another way, the fractional contribution to the expected square in allele-frequency change from drift is $$ \frac{\sigma^{2}(\delta p)}{\sigma^{2}(\delta p)+(\Delta p)^{2}}\simeq\frac{1/N_{e}}{1/N_{e}+\bar{\imath}^{2}h^{2}/n}=\frac{1}{1+\bar{\imath}^{2}h^{2}N_{e}/n} $$ (18.45c) or $$ \frac{\sigma^{2}(\delta p)}{\sigma^{2}(\delta p)+(\Delta p)^{2}}\simeq1-\bar{\imath}^{2}h^{2}N_{e}/n,\quad\mathrm{w h e n}\quad n\gg N e $$ (18.45d)
